data.title: LGI homes raises 2026 gross margin outlook to 18.5%-20.5% as it targets 4,600-5,400 closings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LGI Homes (LGIH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter played out largely as we expected, reflecting disciplined execution across the organization and steady demand for our homes," said (Chairman & CEO Eric Lipar), adding, "as the quarter progressed, sales activity improved across most of our markets, enabling continued backlog growth."
  • "During the quarter, we delivered a total of 916 homes" and "881 homes contributed directly to our revenue of $320 million," (Chairman & CEO Eric Lipar) said, while noting pricing held: "our average selling price increased nearly 3% to approximately $363,000."
  • (Chairman & CEO Eric Lipar) highlighted orders and buyer fallout: "Net orders were 1,221 homes, and our cancellation rate was 45.6%, driven by buyers who are ultimately unable to qualify for financing," and "our backlog at quarter end was 1,699 homes."
  • "Revenue in the first quarter was $319.7 million based on 881 homes closed at an average sales price of $362,924," said (CFO & Treasurer Charles Merdian), adding channel mix shifted: "111 were through our wholesale channel, representing 12.6% of total closings."
  • (CFO Merdian) pointed to profitability and cost structure: "Our first quarter gross margin was 18.7%," "Adjusted gross margin was 23.4%," and "Combined selling, general and administrative expenses totaled $60.5 million or 18.9% of revenue."
  • (VP Investor Relations & Capital Markets Joshua Fattor) emphasized leverage and liquidity: "We ended the quarter with $1.7 billion of debt outstanding," "resulting in a debt-to-cap ratio of 44.8%," and "Total liquidity at the end of the quarter was $355 million."

Outlook

  • "We are right on track to achieve the full year guidance metrics we provided on our last call," (Chairman & CEO Eric Lipar) said, reiterating: "annual closings between 4,600 and 5,400 homes" and "an average selling price between $355,000 and $365,000" as well as "SG&A as a percentage of revenue between 15% and 16%."
  • (Chairman & CEO Lipar) raised margin guidance: "we are raising our full year gross margin to a range between 18.5% and 20.5% and adjusted gross margin between 22% and 24%."
  • On near-term demand and April cadence, (Chairman & CEO Lipar) said, "We anticipate closing between 400 and 450 in April," and "sales trends in April have been similar to March."

Financial results

  • (CFO Merdian) described earnings and unusual taxes: "Pretax net income was $4.3 million," "First quarter net income was $2.2 million or $0.09 per basic and diluted share," and "The effective tax rate in the first quarter was 50%, above our outlook" due to "the timing impact of share-based compensation expenses that vested during the quarter."
  • Excluding certain items, (CFO Merdian) said, "Excluding impairment-related charges and associated tax impacts, net income was $5.6 million or $0.24 per basic and diluted share," and profitability measures improved: "Adjusted EBITDA increased 30% to $24.4 million."
  • On mix and non-core contributions, (CFO Merdian) said "Other income was $4.9 million, driven primarily by the sale of 35 currently or previously leased homes and gains coming from the sale of finished lots and commercial land."
  • On inventory and build pace, (CFO Merdian) said, "At March 31, we owned and controlled 59,028 lots" and "During the quarter, we started 1,137 homes" amid a strategy of "aligning land investment with current sales trends."

Q&A

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked what drove the gross margin upside and higher full-year margin outlook; (Chairman Lipar) responded, "we're seeing cost relief consistently throughout the quarter" and "we were able to push pricing in a number of select communities," adding, "it was prudent to raise gross margin for the year."
  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked whether March rates/geopolitics hit demand and how April is tracking; (Chairman Lipar) said, "There does not seem to be an impact because of war or higher rates" and "We anticipate closing between 400 and 450 in April."
  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: pressed on whether one factor dominated the margin upside and the setup for Q2 margins; (Chairman Lipar) said, "it's all played a factor" and "generally, we expect the second quarter adjusted gross margin to be similar to first."
  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: asked about operational impacts of 40%+ cancellations; (Chairman Lipar) said, "The emphasis should be on our closing guide and the closing guide remains same," and described a tactic of keeping buyers engaged longer: "we have been working with customers" as they "save up for down payment or working on paying off some debt."
  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital: asked if time-to-close is increasing and whether move-up demand is improving; (Chairman Lipar) said "time to close... is going to be elevated" as more buyers contract earlier in construction, and added, "we are seeing success in our Terrata brand."
  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP: asked if regional ASP strength could lift the year and about cost inflation; (Chairman Lipar) said mix and new communities matter, and warned, "we don't really forecast costs going down," while (CFO Merdian) added, "we have 13,000 finished vacant lots" and "feel pretty confident" in lot-cost positioning.
  • Alex Barrón, Housing Research Center: asked about higher backlog/order ASP, wholesale order mix, and other income modeling; (Chairman Lipar) said backlog ASP was "primarily because of the results in the West," while (CFO Merdian) said other income has been "around the $5 million number" recently but is "pretty variable."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive with repeated focus on margin durability and demand sensitivity, including "gross margin, better than you guys were anticipating" (Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research) and "a lot of focus on the gross margin" (Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing control and execution with phrases such as "we are encouraged" (Chairman Lipar) and "we believe we are executing well" (Chairman Lipar), while consistently attributing cancellations to financing qualification.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from Q4’s "results were below the guidance ranges provided" (Chairman Lipar, Q4) to Q1’s "raising our full year gross margin" (Chairman Lipar), while analysts kept pressing on the same core themes of margin drivers and buyer affordability.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 featured a margin guide increase ("raising our full year gross margin" to "18.5% and 20.5%"), compared with Q4’s initial 2026 margin outlook ("gross margin between 18% and 20%" and "adjusted gross margin between 21% and 23%") provided in February.
  • Q1 results reflected a higher reported cancellation rate ("45.6%") than Q4’s ("43.3%"), while management’s explanation stayed consistent around qualification: Q4 cited cancellations as "strictly the ability to get financing" (Chairman Lipar, Q4) and Q1 again said cancellations were "driven by buyers who are ultimately unable to qualify for financing" (Chairman Lipar).
  • Analysts’ focus remained anchored on gross margin and incentives in both quarters, but Q1 questioning expanded into near-term monthly cadence ("We anticipate closing between 400 and 450 in April") while Q4 spent more time on wholesale/institutional policy risk (Chairman Lipar, Q4: "New orders right now are somewhat on pause until we get more clarification on the policy").

Risks and concerns

  • (Chairman & CEO Lipar) flagged affordability and macro sensitivity: "affordability and consumer confidence remain important considerations for buyers, particularly in a volatile rate environment," and the company continues using "targeted price discounts and financing strategies" to support demand.
  • Financing qualification risk remained central, with (Chairman Lipar) citing a "cancellation rate" driven by buyers unable to qualify and describing continued effort to move buyers through underwriting by working on "down payment" and "paying off debt."
  • (VP Fattor) reiterated leverage reduction intent: "We remain focused on reducing leverage" by "work[ing] through older inventory" and "selectively monetize lot positions."

Final takeaway

LGI Homes’ management framed Q1 as tracking expectations with improving sales activity into spring, a growing backlog, and stable community pace, while raising full-year margin guidance after Q1 margins came in ahead of prior expectations. Leadership continued to describe affordability and financing qualification as the key constraints behind elevated cancellations and longer time-to-close, and positioned incentives, inventory actions, and a largely on-balance-sheet land pipeline as core tools to support full-year targets for closings, community count, and pricing.

data.title: NexPoint residential reaffirms 2026 core FFO of $2.42 to $2.71 as it projects concessions to decline 75% by 2H
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NexPoint Residential Trust (NXRT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Net loss for the first quarter was $6.8 million or $0.27 per diluted share on total revenue of $63.5 million." (CFO Paul Richards)
  • "We have always been transparent that 2026 carries a meaningful interest expense headwind as certain swap positions fall off." (CFO Richards) "Full year '26 interest expense is now projected at $69.3 million versus $67.1 million in our original model." (CFO Richards) "Interest rate swaps currently fixed the rate on $917.5 million or 62% of floating rate mortgage debt." (CFO Richards)
  • "2026 guidance reaffirmed. We are reaffirming our full year 2026 core FFO guidance range of $2.42 to $2.71 per diluted share as well as our same-store NOI range of negative 0.5% at the midpoint." (CFO Richards)
  • "During the first quarter, NXRT completed 252 full and partial upgrades, leased 225 upgraded units, achieving an average monthly rent premium of $69 and a 19% ROI." (CFO Richards)
  • "The multifamily deliveries in 2026 will be at their lowest level since 2014." (Chief Investment Officer Matthew McGraner) "We expect fundamentals to stabilize and then accelerate as the back half of 2026 unfolds." (CIO McGraner)
  • "Our AI-powered leasing platform processed 31,882 leads and converted them into 1,571 signed leases during the quarter, a 4.9% lead-to-lease conversion rate versus the industry benchmark of 3.2%." (CIO McGraner) "Self-guided touring technology enabled 24.7% of our leases to be executed after business hours." (CIO McGraner)

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our full year 2026 core FFO guidance range of $2.42 to $2.71 per diluted share." (CFO Paul Richards) "Our core FFO and same-store guidance range is unchanged." (CFO Richards)
  • "Same-store rental income growth of 0% to positive 1.9% with a midpoint of 0.9%." (CFO Richards) "Same-store revenue growth of positive 0.1% to positive 2% with a midpoint of 1.1%." (CFO Richards) "Same-store NOI growth of negative 2.5% to positive 1.5% with a midpoint of negative 0.5%." (CFO Richards)
  • "Since we issued initial guidance in February, the forward SOFR curve has shifted 7 to 47 basis points higher across the remaining quarters of '26." (CFO Richards) "This adds approximately $2.2 million or roughly $0.08 per diluted share of incremental interest expense versus our original assumptions." (CFO Richards)
  • "Our full year 2026 operating forecast projects concession utilization declining 75% from Q1 levels by the second half of the year." (CIO Matthew McGraner) "We forecast Q2 at 1% of GPR, Q3 at 50 basis points and Q4 at 40 basis points." (CIO McGraner)

Financial Results

  • "We reported Q1 core FFO of $17.3 million or $0.68 per diluted share." (CFO Paul Richards) "Total NOI was $37.6 million across 36 properties." (CFO Richards)
  • "On a same-store basis... total income was $61.4 million, down 2.2% year-over-year." (CFO Richards) "Same-store occupancy closed the quarter at 93.6%." (CFO Richards)
  • "On the expense side, same-store operating expenses improved 1.6% year-over-year." (CFO Richards) "Insurance... we settled rates for our new policy renewal on April 1, achieving 13.3% reduction year-over-year." (CFO Richards)
  • "Total concessions were approximately $1.15 million in the quarter, up from $271,000 in Q1 of 2025." (CIO Matthew McGraner) "However, 39% of the year-over-year increase or $342,000 was driven by a single asset, Avant at Pembroke Pines." (CIO McGraner)
  • "We achieved 55 basis points of gross potential rent in Q1" for bad debt, "down 45.7% year-over-year from 1.02% of GPR." (CIO McGraner)

Q&A

  • Michael Lewis, Truist Securities: asked about narrowing the gap between leased and occupied and improving retention; CIO McGraner: "We definitely see an opportunity to continue to drive renewals and also retention, particularly as you get into the summer months." CFO Richards: "The more leases we can sign, I think the better pricing dynamics we have."
  • Michael Lewis, Truist Securities: asked if Q1 and occupancy were ahead of expectations; CIO McGraner: "we missed our NOI budget by $0.25 million or $300,000" but "I personally was pleased with... the trajectory and the trends in occupancy." VP Bonner McDermett: "we'd love to squeeze 10, 20 basis points higher on occupancy" and called bad debt "a real win."
  • Michael Lewis, Truist Securities: pressed for details on fee income offsetting higher rates; VP McDermett: "NXRT would sponsor the DST program" and "we could be a lender to the transaction and make a spread above our credit line, 300 to 400 basis point spread there" while "The sponsor typically takes acquisition fees... 1% to 2% of the gross purchase price of the deal."
  • Buck Horne, Raymond James: asked about real estate taxes outlook; VP McDermett: "we got a couple of wins on the board" and "the outlook is kind of in the 3% to 4% range." Buck Horne, Raymond James: asked about elevated repairs and maintenance; VP McDermett: "we got hit by a couple of kind of onetime things" and "we think R&M broadly stabilizes."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative to neutral, with questions focused on lease/occupancy mechanics, expense lines (taxes and R&M), and how fee income could offset rate headwinds, including Michael Lewis asking, "Is there anything more to say about how that's kind of offsetting this year?"
  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing improving monthly leasing momentum and operational tools, including CIO Matthew McGraner saying, "We believe Q1 was the trough for concession deployment in this cycle."
  • Versus last quarter, management repeated the “second half” inflection framing but added more specificity on concessions and private-capital/DST monetization, including "a credible source of incremental earnings optionality potentially in the range of $0.10 to $0.20 of core FFO over the next 12 months." (CIO McGraner)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language stayed consistent with Q4 2025 initial ranges, with the Q1 2026 call stating, "We are reaffirming our full year 2026 core FFO guidance range of $2.42 to $2.71" (CFO Paul Richards), while Q4 2025 introduced the same core FFO range and a same-store NOI midpoint of "negative 0.5%" (CFO Richards).
  • Interest-rate framing shifted from waiting on swap-market pricing in Q4 to quantifying updated pressure in Q1, including "the forward SOFR curve has shifted 7 to 47 basis points higher" and "Full year '26 interest expense is now projected at $69.3 million." (CFO Richards)
  • Q4 emphasized cautious optimism for a Sunbelt “inflection” and supply math; Q1 reiterated the supply thesis while leaning more heavily into operating-tech outputs and concession normalization, including "Our AI-powered leasing platform processed 31,882 leads" (CIO McGraner) and "concession utilization declining 75% from Q1 levels" in 2H. (CIO McGraner)
  • Analyst focus moved from dividend coverage/buybacks and early-year leasing updates in Q4 to leased-vs-occupied spread, retention, taxes/R&M cadence, and DST fee-income mechanics in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • "2026 carries a meaningful interest expense headwind" and "certain swap positions fall off." (CFO Paul Richards) "We do not attempt to forecast rates, we manage the risk." (CFO Richards)
  • "Total concessions were approximately $1.15 million" and management attributed concentration risk to "a single asset, Avant at Pembroke Pines" amid a "competitive supply wave." (CIO Matthew McGraner)
  • "We incurred elevated onetime costs associated with lender-required CapEx at select Florida properties." (CFO Richards) "These are episodic expenses... and do not reflect a structural change in our cost base." (CFO Richards)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a period of stabilizing operations while keeping full-year 2026 guidance intact, despite higher projected interest expense from a higher forward SOFR curve. Executives highlighted improving leasing momentum into April, a plan for sharply lower concession usage by the second half, and operating-cost and credit benefits tied to centralized operations and AI-enabled leasing and screening. Management also pointed to potential fee and lending income from the adviser’s DST platform as a source of incremental earnings optionality while reiterating expectations for a second-half fundamental improvement as new multifamily supply falls materially.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hope Bancorp targets over 20% 2026 loan growth as it plans MANUBANK deal close in 2H 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hope Bancorp (HOPE) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Our first quarter 2026 results reflected strong year-over-year growth in net income, revenue, loans and deposits, driven by organic growth and the strategic benefits of the Territorial Bancorp acquisition," said (Chairman, President & CEO Kevin Kim), while adding, "Quarter-over-quarter, our pre-provision net revenue grew, supported by improved efficiency and continued progress in lowering our cost of deposits."

  • "Net income for the first quarter of 2026 totaled $30 million," said (CEO Kim), adding it was "up 40% year-over-year" and that "quarter-over-quarter, net income decreased from $34 million, reflecting higher provision for credit losses and income taxes partially offset by growth in pre-provision net revenue."

  • "On March 31, 2026, we announced the accretive acquisition of the Commercial Banking unit of SMBC MANUBANK," said (CEO Kim), adding, "We expect the transaction to close in the second half of 2026." He said the deal is expected to add "approximately $2.5 billion" in C&I and CRE loans and "$2.7 billion in deposits" and emphasized deposits are "only approximately 3%" CDs, while also stating the transaction is planned "through an efficient deployment of capital without the issuance of new shares."

  • "During the quarter, we returned capital through a repurchase of approximately 604,000 common shares totaling $7 million," said (CEO Kim), and added, "Our Board of Directors declared a quarterly common stock dividend of $0.14 per share payable on or around May 22, 2026."

  • "Our net interest income totaled $124 million for the first quarter of 2026," said (Executive VP & CFO Julianna Balicka), adding that net interest margin "was 2.90%, unchanged quarter-over-quarter" and that the cost of average interest-bearing deposits "decreased 77 basis points to 3.37%."

Outlook

  • "We present our updated management outlook for the full year 2026, including the preliminary impact of the pending MANUBANK transaction," said (CEO Kim), adding, "we expect loan growth of over 20% between December 31, 2025, and December 31, 2026."

  • "Relative to our assumptions at the beginning of the year, we are moderating CRE loan growth ahead of the transaction close to manage pro forma loan concentration," said (CEO Kim), while also stating, "we anticipate commercial and residential mortgage loan growth will continue to be robust in 2026."

  • "We anticipate year-over-year total revenue growth to be at the higher end of our 15% to 20% range for the full year of 2026 assuming 1 quarter of contribution from the pending MANUBANK transaction," said (CEO Kim), and added, "We assume no Fed funds target rate cuts in 2026."

  • "We anticipate unchanged pre-provision net revenue growth excluding notable items, at a range of 25% to 30% for the full year 2026," said (CEO Kim), and added, "We anticipate the benefits of cost savings from the Man Bank transaction will begin from 2027" and that management "project[s] the MANUBANK transaction to be meaningfully accretive in to 2027 earnings."

Financial results

  • "Pre-provision net revenue for the first quarter totaled $47 million," said (CEO Kim), adding it was "up 43% year-over-year" and "up 1% quarter-over-quarter."

  • "Noninterest income totaled $17 million" in Q1, said (CFO Balicka), and attributed the linked-quarter decline to "less gains on the sale of investment securities and lower customer level swap fee income."

  • "During the first quarter of 2026, we sold $53 million of SBA loans," said (CFO Balicka), adding, "we recognized SBA gains on sale of $3 million."

  • "Our noninterest expense totaled $94 million in the first quarter of 2026, down from $99 million in the fourth quarter of 2025," said (CFO Balicka), adding, "The efficiency ratio for the first quarter of 2026 improved to 67%."

  • "Criticized loans totaled $325 million, down 7% quarter-over-quarter and down 28% year-over-year," said (CFO Balicka), while also stating, "Net charge-offs were $11 million" and "the 2026 first quarter provision for credit losses was $9 million up from $7 million" in Q4 2025.

Q&A

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked about "the forward appetite" for buybacks and capital targets; (CEO Kim) responded, "That will depend on capital generation and growth opportunities" and added, "Our use of capital to repurchase our shares will be opportunistic."

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked for "the purchase accounting benefit"; (CFO Balicka) said "Not material" and added it is "about similar" quarter-over-quarter at "$4 million," describing it as "a steady benefit each quarter for a number of years."

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: asked whether the lower expense run rate is "sustainable"; (CFO Balicka) said Q1 had "good expense control" but added "the expenses will tick up" as production and revenue strengthen, while staying within prior full-year framing.

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: asked if HOPE is "opting out of the CECL double account" with the acquisition; (CFO Balicka) responded, "We are still going to evaluate."

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: asked about deposit cost outlook; (CFO Balicka) said, "each quarter, we see about 5 to 7 basis points of interest-bearing deposit cost reduction" and added, "we already adopted the ASU for Territorial transaction."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked about moderating CRE and organic mix; (CFO Balicka) said, "I would expect organic loan growth to be mid-single digits" driven by "C&I and residential mortgage" and added, "I would expect flat CRE balances."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked for MANUBANK pro forma CRE concentration; (CFO Balicka) said, "It will be something in the 320% range" and added, "we are planning for organically growing into that."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked what timing is embedded for MANUBANK in guidance; (CFO Balicka) said management is "just plugging in a close at the midpoint of the second half of the year" for "simple arithmetic."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked about elevated charge-offs and what is being watched; (Senior EVP & COO Peter Koh) said, "a lot of these represent sort of previously identified credit concerns that we are cleaning up right now" and added, "overall, we feel very good about asset quality."

  • Timothy Coffey, Brean Capital: asked for new-loan yields; (CFO Balicka) said, "The yields on the new loans were approximately 6.4%." Coffey also asked about margin expectations; (CFO Balicka) said if Fed funds stay flat and deposit costs keep improving, "then you would see margin expansion" as low-yielding CRE loans "continue to mature and reprice to market rates."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly skeptical, pressing on capital return, expense sustainability, CRE concentration management, and credit normalization, including direct asks on buybacks, CECL treatment, and what is embedded in guidance timing.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing optionality and monitoring, including "opportunistic" buybacks (CEO Kim) and "We are still going to evaluate" on CECL (CFO Balicka).

  • Versus last quarter, the current call featured more transaction-driven questioning and modeling clarification tied to MANUBANK timing and concentration management, rather than the prior quarter’s heavier focus on deposit spot rates and CD repricing schedules.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest strategic shift versus Q4 2025 was the introduction of the pending MANUBANK acquisition, which management said is expected to add "$2.5 billion" of loans and "$2.7 billion" of deposits and be "meaningfully accretive" in 2027, alongside an expectation to close in 2H 2026.

  • Guidance framing changed from Q4’s assumption of "two Fed funds target rate cuts" in 2026 to Q1’s outlook that "assume[s] no Fed funds target rate cuts in 2026" (CEO Kim).

  • Analyst focus broadened from Q4’s emphasis on deposit pricing details and hiring cadence to Q1’s emphasis on capital return appetite, pro forma CRE concentration, and transaction timing assumptions.

Risks and concerns

  • "We are moderating CRE loan growth ahead of the transaction close to manage pro forma loan concentration," said (CEO Kim), signaling concentration management as a key planning constraint tied to the MANUBANK balance sheet.

  • "Net charge-offs were $11 million" and the provision was "$9 million," said (CFO Balicka), while (COO Koh) described the drivers as "previously identified credit concerns" being resolved.

  • Noninterest income sensitivity to market activity was highlighted as "lower customer level swap fee income" due to "less underlying transaction activity" (CFO Balicka).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 around year-over-year earnings growth, improved efficiency, and continued funding-cost progress, while positioning the pending MANUBANK acquisition as a major balance-sheet and strategic catalyst expected to close in the second half of 2026, add $2.5 billion of loans and $2.7 billion of deposits with low CD mix, and become meaningfully accretive in 2027. For 2026, management guided to over 20% loan growth and revenue growth at the higher end of the 15% to 20% range (assuming one quarter of MANUBANK contribution), while also emphasizing CRE growth moderation ahead of closing to manage pro forma concentrations and maintaining an effective tax rate outlook of 20% to 25%.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tronox cut to Sell at Truist with limited justification for further upside
data.description:
Tronox (TROX) down 3.3% in Tuesday's trading as Truist downgraded the manufacturer of TiO2 pigment to Sell from Hold with an $8 price target, down from $9, saying it believes the near-term earnings outlook remains challenged with net leverage likely to peak at ~12x this year.

Truist's Peter Osterland said he expects Tronox's (TROX) Q1 results will come in roughly in line with consensus, but he sees risk that the company's guidance will call for a slower degree of sequential earnings improvement this year than modeled by Wall Street; the analyst's Q2 EBITDA estimate is $73M, which would be down 22% Y/Y and 17% below consensus, and he believes a large miss for Q2 guidance could "level-set expectations and drive a reversal for some of the recent share price momentum."

Osterland also estimated that lower Y/Y earnings generation could drive Tronox's (TROX) net leverage as high as ~12x by mid-year; while the nearest debt maturity is not until 2029, he does not think the company's debt load will make it increasingly vulnerable to the potential for the timing and magnitude of global demand recovery in its key end markets to be pushed further to the right.

The analyst continues to see potential for Tronox (TROX) to leverage the value of rare earth elements produced through the company's mineral sands operations as a long-term earnings catalyst, but he said the benefits likely are already priced in.

data.title: Ametek near purchase of CD&R-owned industrial company - WSJ
data.description:
Ametek (AME) is in exclusive discussions to buy part of an industrials company that's owned by private-equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice. Shares of Ametek fell 1.6%.

The deal for Indicor's testing and measurement businesses would value them at about $5 billion, according to a WSJ report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. A deal may happen in the coming weeks, though it's still possible talks fail. 

Indicor may separately sell its flow control and water solutions businesses, according to the report.

Ametek, which has a market cap of $53 billion, is expected to report Q1 results on Thursday.

data.title: Amazon Web Services launches OpenAI's Codex on Bedrock
data.description:
Amazon Web Services (AMZN) has expanded its partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI) and is now offering Codex along with the startup's latest AI models through Amazon Bedrock.

This means AWS customers can now access OpenAI models alongside competing models from Anthropic (ANTHRO), Meta (META), Mistral, Cohere, and others through a single service.

The companies are also partnering to deliver Amazon Bedrock Managed Agents powered by OpenAI's models. This is developed to help enterprises deploy and run agents at scale.

"It is built with the OpenAI agent harness, which is engineered to unlock the full potential of OpenAI frontier models, delivering faster execution, sharper reasoning, and reliable steering of long-running tasks," AWS said. "Security and governance are built in from the moment you deploy: every agent operates with its own identity, logs every action for auditability, and runs inside your environment with all model inference on Amazon Bedrock. As customers scale to hundreds of thousands of agents across the enterprise, they benefit from the globally scalable AWS infrastructure, and proximity to the data, applications, and services they already rely on."

AWS said these are the first steps of deeper collaboration with OpenAI. These offerings are available now in limited preview.

data.title: Corning plans Springboard upgrade through 2030 while forecasting Q2 sales about $4.6B and EPS $0.73-$0.77
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corning Incorporated (GLW) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Year-over-year, sales grew 18% to $4.35 billion. EPS grew 30% to $0.70. Operating margin expanded 220 basis points to 20.2%.” (Chairman, President & CEO Wendell Weeks)

  • “Now based on increasing demand for our innovations, we actually plan to upgrade again and extend our plan through 2030 at our investor event in New York City on May 6.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “We now have concluded two more large, long-term agreements with hyperscale customers, and they are each similar in size and duration to the Meta agreement.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “Our ramp is running behind our ambitious plans. Our wafer facility will undergo an extended maintenance shutdown, and we will transition to a permanent power system and repair and upgrade production equipment to increase throughput in future quarters.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “We delivered our eighth consecutive quarter of year-over-year sales growth while continuing to enhance the financial profile of the company.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Edward Schlesinger)

  • “Today, we announced changes to our segment reporting effective first quarter 2026, which better align with our current operating and management structure.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

Outlook

  • “In the second quarter, we expect to grow sales about 14% year-over-year to approximately $4.6 billion and to grow EPS about 25% year-over-year to a range of $0.73 to $0.77.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “Our second quarter forecast includes an additional $30 million of expense in Q2 versus Q1 as our solar wafer plant undergoes an extended maintenance shutdown.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “For the full year, we expect to generate significantly more free cash flow year-over-year while continuing to invest strongly in our growth vectors aided by customer financial support.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • The provided analysts’ estimates data could not be validated to the required fiscal-quarter format described in the prompt, so no estimates comparisons are included.

Financial results

  • “Year-over-year, in Q1, sales grew 18% to $4.35 billion and EPS increased 30% to $0.70 per share, both coming in at the high point of our guidance.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “In Optical Communications, sales were $1.8 billion, up 36% year-over-year, driven by robust demand for Gen AI products. Net income was $387 million, up 93% year-over-year.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “In Solar, sales were $370 million, up $164 million or 80% year-over-year. Net income was $7 million, down $20 million year-over-year.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “Our first quarter actuals included about a $0.04 EPS impact as we continue to bring up solar wafer capacity to meet committed demand.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “In the quarter, OpEx was $823 million. Included in Q1 OpEx was higher variable compensation expense, including stock-based compensation.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

Q&A

  • John Roberts, Mizuho: “On the new hyperscaler agreements, are there material glass fiber draw capacity expansions associated with that?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “You’re going to see expansion across all of our major Optical operations, including expanding our fiber operations.”

  • John Roberts, Mizuho: “When you complete and you're fully ramped on solar, what would be the approximate breakdown between semiconductor wafers and modules?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “We’re running at about $0.5 billion semiconductor business.” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “Wafer and module. Both of those.”

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: “How tight are you seeing the current state? Are you able to meet ... demand? And how are you seeing the evolution of pricing?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “The pricing environment is clearly favorable for those who have capacity.”

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: “Are we entering an environment in Optical where you could eclipse ... margins greater than display?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “So the simple answer is, yes.”

  • Joshua Spector, UBS: “Do you expect to have a new margin target that you're going to put out there next week?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “Hopefully, you’ll tune in ... and we can talk about all the impacts of our financial profile.”

  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: “Are you able to kind of within these contracts, raise prices over the long term?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “What we’re mainly focused on here is improving visibility.”

  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: “When should we expect the drag on these expenses to be completed...?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “When you take that in aggregate, it’s probably close to $0.07 of EPS in the second quarter guide that we gave.”

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: “How should we think about what that actually implies?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “The simple answer is yes, all of the above.”

  • Meta Marshall, Morgan Stanley: “Are you guys starting to see some of the BEAD demand?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “BEAD is still quite small.”

  • Brendan Rogers, for Wolfe: “Can you guys share any more details on kind of the split between Carrier and Enterprise growth rates?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “Both Carrier and Enterprise grew 36% year-over-year in Q1.”

  • Martin Yang, Oppenheimer: “You haven't raised the CapEx plan despite the two new agreements.” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “CapEx would be about $1.7 billion. We could be a little above that number this year.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical and detail-seeking on capacity, pricing, and contract structure, including “sharing the risk” (JPMorgan) and whether the market is seeing “price increases” (BofA).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and confident on optical demand and profitability, including “the simple answer is, yes” on optical margin potential (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks), while more cautious on solar wafer timing, including “calling the exact timing ... is very hard to do” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management shifted from describing deals “in the process of concluding” to “concluded two more” hyperscaler agreements (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks), and from a 2028-focused Springboard upgrade to “upgrade again and extend ... through 2030” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic scope expanded from “add $11 billion in incremental annualized sales by the end of 2028” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q4 call) to “upgrade again and extend our plan through 2030” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q1 call).

  • Customer-contract momentum in optical advanced from “We are concluding similar long-term agreements with other major customers” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q4 call) to “We now have concluded two more large, long-term agreements with hyperscale customers” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q1 call).

  • Solar narrative shifted from ramp costs being “the primary drag on net income” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger, Q4 call) to a specific operational disruption and cost, including “an extended maintenance shutdown” and “$30 million of additional expense versus the first quarter” in Q2 guidance (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks).

Risks and concerns

  • “Our ramp is running behind our ambitious plans.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “It impacts our margin, it impacts our EPS and it also reduces our sales because we’re shut down for a period of time here.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “We expect memory prices to significantly impact the market in 2026.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a Springboard “proof point” driven by Optical Communications and Solar, highlighted two additional hyperscaler long-term agreements similar to the Meta deal, and guided Q2 to about $4.6 billion in sales and $0.73 to $0.77 EPS while absorbing $30 million of incremental solar wafer expense tied to an extended shutdown aimed at improving future throughput. The company also said it will present an upgraded and extended Springboard plan through 2030 at its May 6 investor event, alongside more detail on Gen AI and a new Photonics market-access platform.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IAC changes name, shifting focus to publishing and MGM Resort investment
data.description:
IAC (IAC) is changing its name to People Incorporated amid a focus on its publishing business and investment in MGM Resorts International (MGM).

In a letter to shareholders, IAC Chairman Barry Diller announced the name change and the company’s history, which evolved from Silver King Communications to IAC/InterActiveCorp, a conglomerate of more than 200 companies and 100 minority investments, including a 26% investment in MGM Resorts (MGM).

With the name change, the company will transition the “necessary” staff of IAC into People Incorporated and “significantly reduce overhead” to concentrate on the two assets,” People publishing and holdings in MGM Resorts (MGM).

While Diller did not directly address any reduction in workforce, he intimated as such given a downsizing to a smaller company.

“The corpus of People Incorporated will include the assets of a mostly virtual media business together with the very hard assets of MGM Resorts—if you like, a perfect hedge in a world that is changing so unpredictably fast,” Diller said, adding that he will remain as chairman and senior executive of the new company.

The name change is expected to occur by the company's second quarter earnings in August.

data.title: Two S&P 500 support levels to watch
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) (VOO) is consolidating near all-time highs following a sharp rebound from its March lows, but the index’s next test may hinge more on defending gains than reaching new peaks.

Liz Thomas, head of investment strategy at SoFi, on Tuesday identified two critical technical support zones to watch if the rally starts to cool. 

The first sits around 6,975 to 7,000, where a 23.6% Fibonacci retracement coincides with a psychologically significant round number and former resistance level. This zone will determine whether the market can successfully turn an old ceiling into a new floor. SP500 was changing hands at 7,136 at press time.

The deeper support zone lies between 6,780 and 6,850, aligning the 38.2% retracement with the 50-day moving average—a level that has proven significant repeatedly over the past seven months, Thomas noted.

data.title: S&P 500 hides weakness as equal-weight index looks to log a 6-day slide
data.description:
U.S. equities are showing signs of underlying weakness despite the broader market’s seemingly stable performance, as a divergence in index structure points to a more fragile backdrop.

The equal-weight S&P 500 (SP500), which offers a clearer view of average stock performance, is on track for its sixth straight daily decline—marking its longest losing streak since December 2024.

This downturn suggests that selling pressure is becoming more widespread beneath the surface, even as headline indexes remain relatively resilient. Since the market’s rebound from its March 30 low, gains have appeared broadly distributed in absolute terms. However, a closer look reveals that much of the upward momentum has been driven by a smaller group of large-cap names. 

The traditional cap-weighted S&P 500 continues to outperform, benefiting disproportionately from strength in mega-cap stocks. Meanwhile, the equal-weighted version still trades below its January peak, underscoring the uneven nature of the recovery. 

Together, these dynamics highlight a hidden correction, where internal market breadth deteriorates even as top-line index performance masks the extent of underlying weakness.

S&P 500 Funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).
